agent1_snippets.title: Cervical Traction -- Technique or Treatment -- Mechanical Cervical Traction
agent1_snippets.content: Mechanical traction includes pinning, with the placement of a Halo device around the head; where anterior pins are placed 1 cm above each of the eyebrows, and two posterior pins are placed on the opposite end of the skull. The addition of pins can be essential if further stabilization is required. A harness attaches to the head and neck of the patient while he is laying down on his/her back. The harness is itself attached to a machine that applies a traction force, which can be regulated through a control panel.

agent1_snippets.title: Cervical Traction -- Technique or Treatment -- Mechanical Cervical Traction
agent1_snippets.content: Mechanical traction requires a 0-degree angle pull for C1 and C2 pathologies, and a 20-degree angle flexion for below C2 cases. Moreover, the force applied during pull tension must not exceed 10 lb (4.5 kg) in cases of C1-C2 subluxation, but can otherwise increase up to 45 lb (20.4 kg). Some practices require a gradual increase of the pull tension, while others prefer choosing the lowest weight inciting an effective response.

agent1_snippets.title: Cervical Traction -- Technique or Treatment -- Over-the-Door Traction
agent1_snippets.content: Furthermore, intermittent traction is another modality where a repeated sequence of rest and traction is applied. It is believed to increase blood flow to the nerve roots and spine parenchyma. One must understand that during the rest phase, tension is not entirely released. As a general rule, intermittent traction is the method of choice for degenerative disc disease and/or joint hypomobility. On the other hand, sustained traction is most often used for neck pain of muscle or soft tissue etiology, and/or disc herniations. Cervical traction can be applied while the patient is supine or seated. The supine position is preferred, allowing for more posterior pressure loading. This will ensure cervical muscle relaxation and transmit less pressure on the temporomandibular joint (TMJ). The sitting position is favored only for patients who cannot lay supine for a prolonged period of time, as in cases of patients suffering from reflux esophagitis.

agent1_snippets.title: Orthodontics, Malocclusion -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: There are several methods to treat malocclusion. Palatal expanders are traditionally used before braces treatment to treat a narrow palate, crowding, or crossbite. Rapid palatal expansion (RPE) works by placing a force on the two maxillary bones to split the mid-palatal suture. Palatal expanders are most useful during adolescence, as the maxillary bones become fused during puberty. The expander contains a screw turned daily or as directed by the patient's orthodontist. Every turn places pressure on the maxillary bone, allowing for less than 1 mm of expansion. [22]

agent1_snippets.title: Retrognathia -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: The treatment and management will depend on what the clinician's concerns are and what a patient or their parents desire. Most commonly retrognathia will be identified in children between the ages of four to ten years old and if the child has a dental home, will likely be referred to an orthodontist for evaluation ideally around the ages of seven to eight. Generally, this is a benign condition without any other effects. This will allow the orthodontist to begin tracking the patient with serial cephalometric radiographs and potentially intervene early with non-surgical therapy like headgear or newer intra-oral techniques that have a similar effect in repositioning the mandible. If the child does not have a dental home or has not seen an orthodontist, a referral is recommended. [13] [14]

agent2_snippets.content: Malposition, disproportion or other labor complications are classified as رَحم ناقص التوتر (uterine inertia), فشل تحريض المخاض (failure of labor induction), مرحلة أولى مطولة من المخاض (prolonged first stage of labor), and مرحلة ثانية مطولة من المخاض (prolonged second stage of labor). agent2_snippets.content: Delivery by double application of forceps is an obstetric intervention that is performed directly on the fetal head. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2026
agent2_snippets.content: Maternal and fetal conditions affecting labor and delivery is a broad category that includes several specific obstetric problems. Among these, breech malpresentation successfully converted to cephalic presentation represents a situation in which a fetus initially in a breech position is turned to the head‑first (cephalic) position before birth, thereby improving the prospects for a vaginal delivery. Cicatrix of cervix affecting pregnancy refers to a cervical scar or fibrous tissue that can compromise cervical integrity and potentially interfere with the progress of pregnancy and the mechanics of labor. Tumor of cervix affecting pregnancy denotes the presence of a cervical neoplasm that may pose risks to both maternal health and fetal development, often necessitating careful monitoring and tailored delivery planning. Additionally, سوء تموضع الجنين (fetal malposition) describes abnormal positioning of the fetus, such as breech or transverse lie, which can impede normal labor progression and may require interventions such as external cephalic version, operative delivery, or cesarean section. Together, these conditions illustrate the diverse ways in which maternal cervical pathology and fetal positioning can impact labor and delivery, underscoring the importance of accurate diagnosis and individualized management to optimize outcomes for both mother and child.

agent2_snippets.content: Surgical Manipulation is the primary obstetric category that integrates therapeutic applications, all forms of obstetrical version (combined, external, internal and manual rotation), a range of assisted‑delivery techniques, cervical and membrane interventions, induction strategies, and pregnancy‑termination procedures, thereby supporting comprehensive ante‑partum, intrapartum and post‑partum care. Combined obstetrical version is a subclass of Surgical Manipulation and serves as the parent of internal and combined version without extraction; internal obstetrical version and external obstetrical version are direct children of Surgical Manipulation. Manual rotation of the fetal head, delivery by double application of forceps, delivery of a vertex presentation, extraction, and vacuum‑assisted delivery (الولادة عن طريق شفط الجنين) are grouped under “other delivery procedures,” classified as بضع الارتفاق (obstetric maneuvers) and as other procedures to assist delivery.
